extra.reasoning:
Enum order: self_care(0) < routine(1) < urgent(2) < emergent(3). The property requires that flipping dizziness from false to true (other inputs fixed) never lowers acuity. The hazard is r3 (priority 100, the highest), which fires only when dyspnea∧fever∧dizziness and forces acuity := self_care (the lowest level).

Take the baseline input dyspnea=true, fever=true, dizziness=FALSE, others false. Matching rules: r6 (fever, prio 3 → emergent) and r4 (dyspnea, prio 2 → urgent). Highest priority is r6, so acuity = emergent.

Now set dizziness=TRUE with all other inputs unchanged (dyspnea=true, fever=true, others false). Now r3 also matches (dyspnea∧fever∧dizziness, prio 100 → self_care), alongside r6 (prio 3 → emergent) and r4 (prio 2 → urgent). The highest-priority matching rule is r3 (priority 100), so acuity = self_care.

Thus setting dizziness true changed acuity from emergent down to self_care — a drop of three levels — violating monotonicity. The witness_assignment is the dizziness=true assignment whose acuity (self_care) is lower than its dizziness=false counterpart (emergent).
